[[BLOCK:slide0_shape0]]
Disadvantages of Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression)

[[BLOCK:slide0_shape1]]
Yu Zhonghao

[[BLOCK:slide1_shape0]]
The Long History of Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression)

[[BLOCK:slide1_shape1]]
Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression), also known as Standard Least Squares Regression (SLS Regression), was discovered by Legendre (1805) and Gauss (1809) during the time of our ancestors.

[[BLOCK:slide2_shape0]]
Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression)

[[BLOCK:slide2_shape1]]
Least Squares = Minimization of Residual (残差 (Residual)) Squares  
Residual (残差 (Residual)) = The distance between observed and predicted values  
Best Fit

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape0]]
R-squared (R平方 (R Square))

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape1]]
The goal of simple regression is to identify a relationship.  
When multiple predictors are present, the relationship is represented by R-squared (R平方 (R Square)) (explained variance).

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape2_shape0]]
Yes

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape2_shape1]]
X

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape2_shape2]]
R-squared (R平方 (R Square))

[[BLOCK:slide4_shape0]]
Ideal Scenario

[[BLOCK:slide4_shape1]]
All predictors are orthogonal.

[[BLOCK:slide5_shape0]]
Inflated Variance Explanation

[[BLOCK:slide5_shape1]]
Imagine the overlapping area between Y and Xs represents the explained variance (multiple relationships).  
As more Xs are added to Y, Y’s circle is almost completely covered. R-squared (R Square) = 0.89! Amazing! A miracle! A divine gift!

[[BLOCK:slide6_shape0]]
Useless Model

[[BLOCK:slide6_shape1]]
A student asked me how to improve their grades. I told them my fifty-variable regression model could predict nearly 89% of exam performance: studying long hours, earning more money, buying a reliable car, watching less TV, going online more often, exercising more, attending church more frequently, praying more often, watching fewer movies, playing fewer video games, getting haircuts more often, drinking more milk and coffee... and so on.  
This complex model is utterly useless!

[[BLOCK:slide7_shape0]]
Stepwise Regression (逐步回归 (Stepwise Regression))

[[BLOCK:slide7_shape1]]
Lavery et al. (2016) stated: “Traditional methods (e.g., bivariate screening, stepwise selection, forward selection) have flaws. The statistics provided in regression output (p-values, parameter estimates, R-squared (R2), etc.) are developed for testing a specific model, and their application is unfounded when used repeatedly as in Stepwise Regression (逐步回归 (Stepwise Regression))” (p. 1).

[[BLOCK:slide8_shape0]]
Stepwise Regression (逐步回归 (Stepwise Regression))

[[BLOCK:slide8_shape1]]
Smith (2018): “Many big data researchers believe that the more potential explanatory variables there are, the more useful Stepwise Regression (逐步回归 (Stepwise Regression)) becomes in selecting explanatory variables. In reality, the more potential explanatory variables, the worse Stepwise Regression performs. Stepwise Regression does not solve the problem of too many explanatory variables in big data. Instead, big data exacerbates the flaws of Stepwise Regression.”  
https://doi.org/10.1186/s40537-018-0143-6

[[BLOCK:slide8_note]]
Smith, G. 2018. Avoid Stepwise Regression (逐步回归 (Stepwise Regression)). Journal of Big Data. 5: 1-12. https://doi.org/10.1186/s40537-018-0143-6.

[[BLOCK:slide9_shape0]]
Goodness of Fit

[[BLOCK:slide9_shape1]]
In this example, I aim to predict weight using six variables from fitness data. The method used is Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression).

[[BLOCK:slide10_shape0]]
Negative Adjusted R-squared (Adjusted R-square)!

[[BLOCK:slide10_shape1]]
R-squared (R Square) is 0.199. Not bad! This model explains 20% of the variance in weight.  
However, when multiple predictors are used, the program adjusts the inflated R-squared using Adjusted R-squared.  
The result is negative! R-squared is r * r, so it shouldn’t be negative. What’s going on?

[[BLOCK:slide11_shape0]]
Negative R-squared (R平方 (R Square))

[[BLOCK:slide11_shape1]]
Zero indicates no explained variance.  
If a straight line is fitted to the data, it will be flat (horizontal).  
If the model performs worse than a horizontal line and contradicts the data pattern, the Adjusted R-squared (R-squared) may be negative.  
Including too many predictors in the model can result in worse performance than having no model at all.  
Analogy: Taking the wrong medication or a placebo when ill may lead to worse outcomes than taking no medication.

[[BLOCK:slide12_shape0]]
Problems with Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression)

[[BLOCK:slide12_shape1]]
Excessive assumptions about Residuals (残差 (Residual)) and predictors  
The model tends to overfit the data.  
The model is unstable when some predictors are highly correlated (Multi-collinearity (多重共线性 (Multi-collinearity))).  
No unique solution exists for large datasets.  
Regarding sample size, the traditional rule of thumb is 1 (predictor):10 (observations); if p > n, Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression) cannot be performed.  
The model must be linear.

[[BLOCK:slide13_shape0]]
Generalized Regression (广义回归 (Generalized Regression))

[[BLOCK:slide13_shape1]]
Proposed by Friedman (2008)  
Similar to abduction or IBE: Instead of being tied to a single answer, consider multiple possibilities.  
There may be multiple solutions to a problem—why not explore different paths?  
Start with no modeling or zero coefficients.  
Try a series of models. The solution is flexible (variable).  
Select the best solution (determined by algorithms, not manually)!

[[BLOCK:slide14_shape0]]
Keep It Simple!

[[BLOCK:slide14_shape1]]
Also known as Regularized Regression (正则化回归 (Regularized Regression)): Aims to reduce variance and improve model generalization to avoid overfitting.  
Also known as Penalized Regression (惩罚回归 (Penalized Regression)): Penalizes overly complex or exaggeratedly fitted models → Keep It Simple, Stupid (KISS) principle!

[[BLOCK:slide15_shape0]]
Loss Function (损失函数 (Loss function))

[[BLOCK:slide15_shape1]]
A function that maps events involving one or more variables to a real number, representing the cost of the event.  
What is the cost if an error occurs in the event? What do you lose?  
In statistics, this cost is the error or Residual (残差 (Residual)).

[[BLOCK:slide16_shape0]]
Loss Function (损失函数 (Loss function)): A Metaphor

[[BLOCK:slide16_shape1]]
A hiker attempts to descend from a mountain peak at dusk. He surveys the surroundings, searching for all possible routes. As he proceeds along one path, he realizes it does not lead to his destination. He abandons this option because it wastes time and energy or incurs too high a cost. After several trials and errors, poor choices are eliminated, and the hiker gradually moves toward a direction of reduced loss. Eventually, he selects the most cost-effective route, or a path with negative cost.

[[BLOCK:slide18_shape0]]
Different Methods of Regularization and Penalization

[[BLOCK:slide19_shape0]]
Regularization

[[BLOCK:slide19_shape1]]
The main difference between Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression) and Penalized Regression (惩罚回归 (Penalized Regression)) is that Penalized Regression adds a regularization term to the Loss Function (损失函数 (Loss function)) to optimize coefficient estimation, hence Penalized Regression is also called Regularized Regression (正则化回归 (Regularized Regression)).  
L1 Regularization (L1正则化 (L1 Regularization)) (LASSO): Uses the sum of the absolute values of coefficient estimates as the regularization term.  
L2 Regularization (L2正则化 (L2 Regularization)) (Ridge Regression (岭回归 (Ridge))): Uses the sum of the squared coefficients as the regularization term.
